headline: Kirkwood Pulse • March 12, 2026
overview:
project_overview:
  developer: Double Eagle Development
  location: Former Public Works site, Kirkwood
  proposal:
  homes: 202
  buildings: 6
  building_height: four-story
  parking_spots: 247
  new_street: through street connecting Taylor to Fillmore
  housing_types:
  -: ranch
  -: townhome
  amenities:
  -: pool
  -: fitness center
  -: lounge
  -: grill stations
  -: professionally maintained grounds
  design_features:
  -: pedestrian friendly streets and sidewalks
  -: gathering spaces
  -: art installations
  -: robust trees and plantings
  -: covered direct entry front porches
  -: private attached garages
  -: architectural elements reflecting historic Kirkwood homes with modern features
  zoning: R-7 Planned Multifamily (new zoning code proposed for project)
criticisms:
  hotel_absence: No hotel included despite original RFP requirement
  retail_exclusion: No ground-floor retail allowed under new zoning; seen as a missed opportunity for neighborhood businesses and trail users
  building_design: Six blocky, mostly identical buildings lacking human scale
  parking: Extensive surface parking reminiscent of suburban developments; no structured parking proposed
  development_phasing: Project not divided for phased development by multiple entities
  city_financials: Unclear how much revenue city will gain to offset $12 million spent on site
positive_notes:
  -: New through street improves connectivity and walkability in Downtown Kirkwood
outlook: Details remain unclear; community and city have significant concerns to address before final approval context: The article discusses the proposals rejected by the Kirkwood City Council for the Public Works site development, following the selection of Double Eagle's proposal.
process: The Public Works RFP process had two stages with initial bids from five applicants (Double Eagle, Keeley, Ridgehouse, Savoy, Mehlman). The Council gave feedback and received revised proposals.
council_preferences:
  -: Include a hotel in the project
  -: Majority owner-occupied residential units
  -: Flexibility on height and density with contextual architectural design
  -: Consider including KPAC surface parking lot with careful phasing
  -: Request incentives to increase purchase price (excluding TIF)
  -: Structured parking preferred to minimize surface parking
  -: Council involvement in architectural review favoring traditional downtown design
proposals_overview:
  Keeley:
  description: Two-stage development with 233 homes including 32 for-sale townhomes, 150-unit multifamily building, 51-unit addition, and a 477-stall parking garage shared with KPAC patrons. Optional third stage for boutique hotel or 24-unit mixed-use building with retail. Included public pocket parks and strong street grid integration.
  strengths: Best integration with Kirkwood’s street grid and public parks.
  Ridgehouse:
  description: 30 for-sale townhomes, two 100-apartment buildings, 9,000 sqft retail, and a 654-space parking garage behind KPAC totaling 760 parking spaces. Did not include a through-street as requested.
  weakness: Lack of through-street as requested in RFP.
  Savoy:
  description: Most retail-heavy proposal with a 100-room hotel on KPAC surface lot, 62 homes (villas, townhomes, mixed-use), ~15,000 sqft commercial space, a new water feature integrated with creek for storm runoff retention, trails and paths.
  strengths: Emphasis on retail and environmental integration.
  Mehlman:
  description: Plan for a 126-room hotel (Marriot A-Loft or similar), 15,000 sqft retail, 27 villas, and 148 parking spots. No revised proposal submitted.
  note: No revised submission after Council feedback.
hotel_inclusion_issue: Despite city leadership emphasis on attracting a hotel downtown, all three hotel-including proposals were rejected. Council feedback indicated that mandatory hotel inclusion lowered the purchase price developers were willing to pay.
additional_notes: Keeley’s detailed responses to Council feedback provide insight into the tradeoffs guiding the process. Full submissions are available via FOIA.

items.title: 202 Homes Proposed for Former Public Works Site
items.excerpt: Well folks, after nearly a year of waiting, the results of the Request for Proposals on the former Public Works site have finally surfaced via this week’s Planning & Zoning agenda. The selected proposal comes from St. Louis-based Double Eagle Development and calls for 202 homes across six four-story buildings, accompanied by 247 parking spots. Double Eagle’s description of the

items.title: Public Works Site: The Rejects
items.excerpt: Last week I wrote up how Double Eagle won the Public Works sweepstakes. Ahead of this Thursday’s City Council public hearing on the Double Eagle proposal, I wanted to explore the other proposals that the Council passed up. The Public Works RFP process unfolded in two stages. Initial bids were submitted by five applicants —Double Eagle, Keeley, Ridgehouse, Savoy, and
items.ai_summary:
context: The article discusses the proposals rejected by the Kirkwood City Council for the Public Works site development, following the selection of Double Eagle's proposal.
process: The Public Works RFP process had two stages with initial bids from five applicants (Double Eagle, Keeley, Ridgehouse, Savoy, Mehlman). The Council gave feedback and received revised proposals.
council_preferences:
  -: Include a hotel in the project
  -: Majority owner-occupied residential units
  -: Flexibility on height and density with contextual architectural design
  -: Consider including KPAC surface parking lot with careful phasing
  -: Request incentives to increase purchase price (excluding TIF)
  -: Structured parking preferred to minimize surface parking
  -: Council involvement in architectural review favoring traditional downtown design
proposals_overview:
  Keeley:
  description: Two-stage development with 233 homes including 32 for-sale townhomes, 150-unit multifamily building, 51-unit addition, and a 477-stall parking garage shared with KPAC patrons. Optional third stage for boutique hotel or 24-unit mixed-use building with retail. Included public pocket parks and strong street grid integration.
  strengths: Best integration with Kirkwood’s street grid and public parks.
  Ridgehouse:
  description: 30 for-sale townhomes, two 100-apartment buildings, 9,000 sqft retail, and a 654-space parking garage behind KPAC totaling 760 parking spaces. Did not include a through-street as requested.
  weakness: Lack of through-street as requested in RFP.
  Savoy:
  description: Most retail-heavy proposal with a 100-room hotel on KPAC surface lot, 62 homes (villas, townhomes, mixed-use), ~15,000 sqft commercial space, a new water feature integrated with creek for storm runoff retention, trails and paths.
  strengths: Emphasis on retail and environmental integration.
  Mehlman:
  description: Plan for a 126-room hotel (Marriot A-Loft or similar), 15,000 sqft retail, 27 villas, and 148 parking spots. No revised proposal submitted.
  note: No revised submission after Council feedback.
hotel_inclusion_issue: Despite city leadership emphasis on attracting a hotel downtown, all three hotel-including proposals were rejected. Council feedback indicated that mandatory hotel inclusion lowered the purchase price developers were willing to pay.
additional_notes: Keeley’s detailed responses to Council feedback provide insight into the tradeoffs guiding the process. Full submissions are available via FOIA.
